behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-module handoff: raw-body/signature verification must gate queue write. A pure unit test of HMAC comparison can pass while wiring still forwards invalid events. No real OS/network/subprocess is required because in-process HTTP harness plus real middleware stack reproduces this. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text does not require real network conditions, only correct local wiring and side-effect suppression. MATRIX-USAGE: Unit vs Integration row settled this.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook route in-process with real raw-body parsing and signature middleware, send a request with mismatched `X-Signature-256`, assert HTTP 401 and assert queue `enqueue`/insert is never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across local modules (HTTP handler -> persistent queue adapter -> response). Minimum real failure appears at module boundary and async sequencing, not a single function. No external infra beyond local persistence is required. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text does not require real network or subprocess behavior; local persistent store integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use real queue persistence backend used in tests (e.g., sqlite/file/local DB). Trigger webhook and instrument timestamps/order: queue write commit completes before response is finalized (or response is withheld until write success).

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure is asynchronous orchestration between enqueue path and downstream worker completion. A unit test of one function can miss cross-module await/Promise chaining mistakes. Real OS/network is not required; in-process wiring can expose accidental blocking on worker completion. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is about local async dependency, not real network/subprocess effects.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with a downstream processor that blocks on a controllable promise. Send valid webhook, assert response returns before promise resolution, then resolve promise and verify processing completes later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a wall-clock latency SLO under concurrency; real HTTP server/event-loop scheduling and timing effects are part of the failure boundary. In-process mocks can hide queue contention/backpressure and timeout behavior. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly requires "within 3 seconds wall-clock" under concurrent load, which depends on runtime/HTTP behavior beyond simple module wiring. MATRIX-USAGE: Integration vs System row settled this.
behaviors.test_description: Start the actual service process (or full app server), pre-load slow downstream work, then send concurrent webhook requests over real HTTP (loopback). Measure end-to-end response times and assert each new request receives response <3s while prior events are still processing.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness depends on state handoff between handler and persistent queue/idempotency store. Unit tests of ID-check logic alone can miss race/order or persistence wiring failures. No real network/subprocess needed; local persistence integration is enough. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text does not require OS/network primitives beyond local storage semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Using real test persistence, send same event ID twice through the handler and assert only one queue record exists (or second enqueue returns duplicate/no-op) and no second processing record is created.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is contract between queue-capacity signal and HTTP mapping (status/header). Multiple modules must interact correctly; single-function unit tests can miss translation/wiring issues. Real OS/network is unnecessary. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because exact behavior can be reproduced in-process by forcing queue-full condition.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue to full (or fill it via test fixture), call webhook route, assert HTTP 503 and `Retry-After` header present with expected value/format, and assert event is not accepted.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This validates queue implementation + persistent storage + config application together; real failure occurs at component/storage boundary, not just pure algorithm logic. Local DB/filesystem-backed queue is sufficient and fits Integration definition. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because no real external network/subprocess requirement is stated; capacity enforcement is local persistence behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with a small capacity, enqueue up to limit, then attempt one more enqueue and assert a capacity error/rejection and unchanged persisted count.
